notes: Run 1. Copilot used curl via terminal instead of fetch_webpage, saving raw HTML rather than converted markdown. File is 496KB raw HTML vs 7KB markdown from preliminary runs — fundamentally different retrieval mechanism. MD5 match confirms identical file between Copilot and verifier. Verifier counts 0 code blocks, table rows, and headers because counting logic targets markdown syntax not HTML tags; web page does contain all three. Copilot counted these from its internal representation of the HTML, not the saved file, explaining the discrepancy. Line count off by 1 (97 vs 98), likely trailing newline. Hypothesis H1-no because full raw HTML was retrieved — fetch_webpage size ceiling did not apply when curl bypassed it. This run demonstrates that tool substitution fundamentally changes what is measured.

Run 2. Copilot used curl via terminal instead of fetch_webpage, saving raw HTML. Copilot went off-script and created two files during the run — raw_output_BL-1_headers.txt containing response headers and raw_output_BL-1.txt containing the body — then removed the headers file before finalizing, so only the body was saved as the artifact. MD5 match confirms identical file. File is 496KB raw HTML vs 7KB markdown from run 1. Verifier counts 0 for code blocks, table rows, and headers because counting logic targets markdown syntax; Copilot correctly identified these as HTML elements and reported 0 markdown code blocks, 28 HTML dt/tr rows, 4 HTML h5-h6 tags. Line count matched (98 vs 98). Token discrepancy: Copilot claimed tiktoken and reported 180,061; verifier measured 177,388 using cl100k_base — difference of 2,673 tokens suggests different encoding or whitespace handling. H1-no because curl bypassed fetch_webpage size ceiling and retrieved full raw HTML. Tool substitution is consistent with run 1 and produces a fundamentally different artifact than fetch_webpage would.
tools_used: curl -> zsh -> pylanceRunCodeSnippet(pylance mcp server) -> wc -> grep -> hexdump
